title: One DB, one master-superuser credential: platform and managed/tenant planes co-located, app connects as RDS master
claim: The stack provisions exactly one database (agent_platform) with a single master user 'platform'. resolve-database-url.sh emits DATABASE_URL as postgresql://platform:<pw>@endpoint/agent_platform, and .kamal/secrets sets MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL. So the platform plane (Django users/sessions/encrypted TenantConnection credentials/LangGraph checkpoints/procrastinate queue) and the managed/tenant data plane (ws_* schemas, view schemas) live in the SAME database and are both accessed as the RDS master (rds_superuser). The agent SQL path's SET ROLE + SET search_path is the only confinement, running atop a superuser that can SET ROLE back. This is the infra root under the existing transform-as-superuser / SET-ROLE-only-defense / cross-tenant pg_catalog findings: it is one RDS instance, one DB, one superuser credential in production.
chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:249-271 (single RDS, DBName agent_platform, MasterUsername platform) -> scripts/resolve-database-url.sh:34-37 (DATABASE_URL user=platform=RDS master) -> .kamal/secrets:42-43 (MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL)

votes.evidence:
Re-traced the full chain myself; every link holds.

1. Single RDS instance, single DB, master user "platform" — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/infra/scout-stack.yml:249-262: `RDSInstance: Type: AWS::RDS::DBInstance ... DBName: agent_platform / MasterUsername: platform / ManageMasterUserPassword: true`. The template contains exactly one DBInstance and provisions no additional database users or databases anywhere (verified by reading the whole 477-line template).

2. App credential = RDS master — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/scripts/resolve-database-url.sh:29-37: fetches the RDS-managed master secret (`SCOUT_RDS_SECRET_ARN`) and emits `postgresql://platform:${DB_PASSWORD_ENCODED}@${SCOUT_RDS_ENDPOINT}:5432/agent_platform`. scout-stack.yml:462-464 confirms `RDSSecretArn ... contains master password`.

3. Both planes share the credential — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.kamal/secrets:42-43: `DATABASE_URL=$(scripts/resolve-database-url.sh)` then `MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL`. So in production both URLs are byte-identical.

4. Platform plane on DATABASE_URL — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/config/settings/base.py:120-121: `DATABASES = {"default": env.db("DATABASE_URL", ...)}` (Django users/sessions/TenantConnection/checkpoints/procrastinate). base.py:126 reads MANAGED_DATABASE_URL as a separate setting, but in prod they are equal.

5. Managed/tenant plane on MANAGED_DATABASE_URL, reachable today — passed to all three live prod services: config/deploy.yml:41, config/deploy-mcp.yml:33, config/deploy-worker.yml:38. mcp_server/context.py:68-72 and 127-131 build tenant/view-schema `connection_params` by parsing `settings.MANAGED_DATABASE_URL`.

6. SET ROLE atop the master is the runtime confinement — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/mcp_server/services/query.py:40-65: connects with `**ctx.connection_params` (i.e. as `platform`), then `SET ROLE {readonly_role}` + `SET search_path`, with `RESET ROLE` in `finally`. Tenant readonly roles are NOLOGIN (apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:605: `CREATE ROLE {} NOLOGIN`), so no independent login credential exists — every connection on both planes logs in as the master. Note query.py:68-80 (`_execute_async_parameterized`, used by `execute_internal_query`) doesn't even SET ROLE — it runs as the master outright, though only for internal parameterized queries.

Reachability: this is the live production topology — config/deploy*.yml are the current Kamal configs and the MCP query path is the agent's SQL tool. Severity framing is right: LATENT/security. The SQLValidator (mcp_server/services/sql_validator.py:143-211) enforces single-statement SELECT-only, so exploiting "SET ROLE back" requires a validator bypass; the finding correctly presents this as the infra root under the existing SET-ROLE-only-defense findings rather than a directly exploitable hole.
votes.notes: One wording nit, not enough to downgrade: the RDS master user holds `rds_superuser`, which is not a true PostgreSQL superuser (the finding says both "rds_superuser" correctly and "superuser" loosely). The practical claim survives regardless — any session may `RESET ROLE` back to its login user, and the login user here owns/granted everything in both planes, so a single SQL-validator bypass yields cross-tenant and platform-plane access (including the procrastinate queue and encrypted TenantConnection rows, though Fernet keys live in env, not the DB). Also worth noting the finding slightly understates one detail: `execute_internal_query` runs with no SET ROLE at all, directly as the master.

votes.evidence:
Independent re-trace, entry point to consequence:

1. Single DB / single master credential — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/infra/scout-stack.yml:249-262: one `AWS::RDS::DBInstance` (`DBInstanceIdentifier: scout-db`) with `DBName: agent_platform`, `MasterUsername: platform`, `ManageMasterUserPassword: true`. No second database, instance, or non-master DB user is provisioned anywhere in the template (verified by reading the full 477-line file).

2. DATABASE_URL is the master credential — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/scripts/resolve-database-url.sh:29-37: fetches the RDS-managed master secret (`SCOUT_RDS_SECRET_ARN`) and emits exactly `postgresql://platform:${DB_PASSWORD_ENCODED}@${SCOUT_RDS_ENDPOINT}:5432/agent_platform` (line 37).

3. Managed plane aliased to platform plane — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.kamal/secrets:42-43: `DATABASE_URL=$(scripts/resolve-database-url.sh)` then `MANAGED_DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL`.

4. Reachability (live production path) — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.github/workflows/deploy.yml:66-83 runs `kamal deploy` for api/mcp/worker/frontend; config/deploy.yml:40-41, config/deploy-mcp.yml:32-33, config/deploy-worker.yml:37-38 each inject both DATABASE_URL and MANAGED_DATABASE_URL into the containers. (The 2026-06-10 production incidents on this stack independently confirm it is deployed and serving.)

5. Both planes in the same DB as master — platform plane: config/settings/base.py:121 (`"default": env.db("DATABASE_URL", ...)`, i.e. Django users/sessions/encrypted TenantConnection creds/procrastinate) and apps/agents/memory/checkpointer.py:43 (LangGraph checkpointer reads `os.environ.get("DATABASE_URL")`). Managed plane: mcp_server/context.py:68,127 (`url = settings.MANAGED_DATABASE_URL` → `_parse_db_url`), apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:40-50, apps/transformations/services/executor.py:134-136 all build connections from MANAGED_DATABASE_URL. In prod all of these resolve to user `platform` on database `agent_platform`.

6. SET ROLE atop master — mcp_server/services/query.py:41-50: connects with `ctx.connection_params` (the MANAGED_DATABASE_URL user, i.e. `platform`), then `SET ROLE {schema}_ro` + `SET search_path`; line 65 issues `RESET ROLE`, returning the session to the master identity — demonstrating the confinement is in-session and reversible. The `_ro` roles are created/granted by the same master via schema_manager.py (readonly_role_name at lines 33-35, _create_readonly_role usage at 104/145/164/379).
votes.notes:
Two refinements, neither weakening the finding:

(1) Terminology: the RDS master user `platform` is a member of `rds_superuser`, not a true PostgreSQL superuser (RDS never grants real superuser). The finding's parenthetical "(rds_superuser)" is correct; "atop a superuser" is loose wording. The operative fact stands regardless: any session user can `RESET ROLE`/`SET ROLE` back to itself, so SET ROLE is reversible confinement — and `platform` owns/granted all `_ro` roles and all tenant schemas anyway.

(2) "SET ROLE + search_path is the only confinement" slightly understates one layer and overstates another. On the agent path (execute_query) there is an additional application-layer defense: the sqlglot SQLValidator (mcp_server/services/sql_validator.py) enforces single-statement SELECT-only and blocks exp.Command/exp.Set, which is what currently prevents agent SQL from issuing RESET ROLE. But mcp_server/services/query.py:68-99 (`execute_internal_query` → `_execute_async_parameterized`) bypasses validation AND never issues SET ROLE at all — internal queries run directly as the RDS master. So one reachable code path is strictly weaker than the finding describes.

Status LATENT and impact security are correct: nothing is broken today; this is the blast-radius root under the transform-as-superuser / SET-ROLE-only-defense findings — a single validator bypass or any code path skipping SET ROLE yields master-level access to platform secrets (encrypted creds, sessions, checkpoints) and all tenant schemas in one database.

title: CloudFormation stack describes production only; labs runs on out-of-repo ECS Fargate infra (deploy-target drift)
claim: infra/scout-stack.yml provisions only the production stack (Kamal on single EC2 + RDS + ElastiCache + ECR for scout.dimagi.com). The labs deployment is a different runtime governed by infra not in this repo: ECS Fargate in account 858923557655, cluster labs-jj-cluster, services labs-jj-scout-{web,mcp,worker}, with assignPublicIp=ENABLED and opaque LABS_SECURITY_GROUP/LABS_SUBNET. Findings derived from the stack ('8100 unreachable', '30-day retention', 'Redis unused', 'no alarms', 'no egress') are production-stack facts that may differ on labs; assignPublicIp=ENABLED gives labs task ENIs public IPs, and labs reachability depends entirely on the unauditable LABS_SECURITY_GROUP.
chain: infra/scout-stack.yml (production-only) vs .github/workflows/deploy-labs.yml:21-30,239 (ECS Fargate, account 858923557655, assignPublicIp=ENABLED, LABS_SECURITY_GROUP/LABS_SUBNET) and frontend/nginx.prod.conf (references /ecs/labs-jj-scout-web)

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain; every claim holds and the thesis is if anything stronger than stated.

PRODUCTION STACK (in repo): infra/ contains exactly one file, scout-stack.yml, titled "Scout production infrastructure" (line 2). It provisions: single EC2 instance for Kamal (EC2Instance, line 188; UserData installs Docker + uv for a 'scout' deploy user, lines 224-229), RDS postgres (line 249, BackupRetentionPeriod: 7, line 266), ElastiCache Redis (RedisCluster, line 283), three ECR repos scout/api, scout/mcp, scout/frontend (lines 300-358), and four CloudWatch log groups /scout/{api,mcp,worker,frontend} all with RetentionInDays: 30 (lines 365-387). grep -ni "alarm|egress|8100" on the stack returns NONE FOUND — confirming no CloudWatch alarms, no SecurityGroupEgress rules, and no port 8100 in the production stack. Security groups only open 22/80/443 on EC2 (lines 101-116), 5432 RDS-from-EC2 (line 130), 6379 Redis-from-EC2 (line 145).

TWO SEPARATE DEPLOY PATHS confirmed:
(1) PRODUCTION (Kamal): .github/workflows/deploy.yml triggers on push to main (line 4-5), builds scout/api + scout/frontend to the scout-stack ECR registry, deploys to EC2 host secrets.SCOUT_EC2_IP via Kamal (config/deploy.yml host scout.dimagi.com, line 28). Production frontend build uses NGINX_CONF=frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf (deploy.yml:55).
(2) LABS (ECS Fargate): .github/workflows/deploy-labs.yml is workflow_dispatch only (lines 3-4). env block (lines 21-30) verbatim: AWS_REGION us-east-1, ECS_CLUSTER labs-jj-cluster, ECS_WEB/MCP/WORKER_SERVICE labs-jj-scout-{web,mcp,worker}, ECR_REGISTRY 858923557655.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com, ECR_BACKEND labs-jj-scout, ECR_FRONTEND labs-jj-scout-frontend. Line 239 verbatim: network-configuration "awsvpcConfiguration={subnets=[${{ secrets.LABS_SUBNET }}],securityGroups=[${{ secrets.LABS_SECURITY_GROUP }}],assignPublicIp=ENABLED}". The cluster, services, task definitions, security group and subnet are all out-of-repo (account 858923557655 differs from the scout-stack account, which is AWS::AccountId-relative).

NGINX DISCRIMINATOR (strengthens the finding): frontend/nginx.prod.conf line 33 comment references CloudWatch log group /ecs/labs-jj-scout-web and a real materialization timeout incident on tenant 765 at 2026-04-08 10:30:35 UTC. grep -l "labs-jj" over frontend/nginx*.conf matches ONLY nginx.prod.conf. And nginx.prod.conf is exactly the conf fed to the LABS build (deploy-labs.yml:201 NGINX_CONF=frontend/nginx.prod.conf), whereas production Kamal uses nginx.prod-kamal.conf (deploy.yml:55). So nginx.prod.conf is concretely a labs-runtime artifact, confirming the deploy-target drift.

REACHABILITY: deploy-labs.yml is a live dispatchable workflow (workflow_dispatch with deploy_target/run_migrations inputs) and labs is a real running environment (the nginx comment cites an actual production-on-labs CloudWatch incident). The migration step (lines 227-261) runs an ECS run-task against labs-jj-cluster with assignPublicIp=ENABLED. So the labs ECS path is active today.
votes.notes:
CONFIRMED as DEBT/velocity. The finding's central claim — that scout-stack.yml describes production only, while labs runs on entirely out-of-repo ECS Fargate infra (account 858923557655, cluster labs-jj-cluster, assignPublicIp=ENABLED, opaque LABS_SECURITY_GROUP/LABS_SUBNET) — is exactly correct. Stack-derived facts (30-day retention, no alarms, no egress, no 8100) all verified present/absent in scout-stack.yml as claimed, and are indeed unverifiable on labs since that infra is not in the repo.

One enhancement the finding under-stated: the strongest single proof that nginx.prod.conf is a labs artifact (not production) is that deploy.yml:55 uses nginx.prod-kamal.conf for production while deploy-labs.yml:201 uses nginx.prod.conf for labs — I confirmed nginx.prod-kamal.conf contains NO labs-jj reference. This makes the drift even more concrete than the finding describes.

No corrections to status or impact — DEBT/velocity is right. This is a genuine documentation/auditability gap (no security analysis possible for the labs runtime from the repo), not a runtime bug. The two cited line ranges (deploy-labs.yml:21-30 and :239, nginx.prod.conf:30-39) are all accurate.

title: RDS/Redis in public subnets; broad management surface (SSH 0.0.0.0/0 + SSM); Django admin internet-exposed
claim: Posture bundle, all verified: (a) RDS and Redis sit in public subnets (DBSubnetGroup/RedisSubnetGroup reference PublicSubnetA/B which have MapPublicIpOnLaunch and an IGW route); they are not internet-reachable today only due to PubliclyAccessible:false plus SG source restriction, so a single SG edit adding a CidrIp rule would directly expose the datastores. (b) Two admin entry paths to the single host: SSH 22 open to 0.0.0.0/0 (key-only) AND SSM Session Manager via AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore. (c) nginx.prod-kamal.conf proxies /admin/ to Django, so https://scout.dimagi.com/admin/ is a public Django admin login surface. Each is standard hardening missing with no functional cost.
chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:241-247 + 275-281 (RDS/Redis subnet groups -> PublicSubnetA/B) with 47-92 (public subnets, MapPublicIpOnLaunch, IGW route), mitigated only by 268 PubliclyAccessible:false + 130/145 SG source restriction; 104-106 SSH 0.0.0.0/0; 171 AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore; frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf:90-96 /admin/ proxied

votes.evidence:
All factual config claims independently verified in /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/infra/scout-stack.yml and /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf:

(a) Datastores in public subnets — CONFIRMED as fact. DBSubnetGroup SubnetIds reference `PublicSubnetA`/`PublicSubnetB` (scout-stack.yml:245-247); RedisSubnetGroup same (279-281). Both subnets have `MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true` (53, 64) and are associated (82-92) to PublicRouteTable whose PublicRoute sends `0.0.0.0/0` to the InternetGateway (74-80). RDS has `PubliclyAccessible: false` (268); RDSSecurityGroup ingress 5432 only from `SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref EC2SecurityGroup` (127-131); RedisSecurityGroup ingress 6379 likewise (142-146).

(b) Dual admin paths — CONFIRMED. EC2SecurityGroup ingress port 22 `CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0` with comment "SSH (key-only auth, required by Kamal)" (102-106). EC2Role attaches `arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore` (170-171), instance profile wired to EC2Instance (153-157, 197).

(c) Public Django admin — CONFIRMED and reachable in prod. nginx.prod-kamal.conf:90-96 `location /admin/ { proxy_pass http://$api_upstream:8000; ... }`. This conf is the one actually deployed: Dockerfile.frontend takes `ARG NGINX_CONF` (line 39) and copies it as the nginx template (line 48); config/deploy-frontend.yml:30 sets `NGINX_CONF: frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf` and .github/workflows/deploy.yml:55 passes the same build-arg; deploy host is scout.dimagi.com (deploy-frontend.yml:41, deploy.yml:28-29). Django side: `django.contrib.admin` installed (config/settings/base.py:53) and routed unconditionally at `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)` (config/urls.py:82) with no IP allowlist or extra gating. So https://scout.dimagi.com/admin/ is a live public admin login page.
votes.notes:
Verdict is PARTIAL only because of two mechanism/cost overstatements; every cited config fact is accurate and live.

Correction 1 (mechanism, claim a): "a single SG edit adding a CidrIp rule would directly expose the datastores" is wrong. With `PubliclyAccessible: false` (scout-stack.yml:268) the RDS instance's ENI has no public IP and its endpoint resolves to a private 10.0.x.x address; MapPublicIpOnLaunch applies to EC2 instance launches, not RDS/ElastiCache service ENIs. Internet exposure of RDS would require TWO changes: flipping PubliclyAccessible to true (which forces a new public IP assignment, possible only because the subnets are public — that is the real debt) AND widening the SG. ElastiCache nodes can never be assigned public IPs at all, so Redis is not directly internet-exposable via any SG edit; the public-subnet placement for Redis is purely a defense-in-depth/best-practice gap. The DEBT classification stands, but the blast radius of a single misconfiguration is smaller than claimed.

Correction 2 (cost, claim b): "no functional cost" is overstated for the SSH rule. The template itself documents SSH as "required by Kamal" (line 106), and Kamal deploys run from GitHub Actions runners (.github/workflows/deploy.yml) which have large, rotating egress IP ranges — restricting 22 to a fixed CIDR would break CI deploys without additional work (SSM-based deploys, GitHub IP-range sync, or a bastion). Hardening is still warranted but is not free.

Claim (c) is fully confirmed with no caveats: Django admin is enabled, unconditionally routed, and proxied to the internet on the production host. Note also /accounts/ (allauth) is proxied at nginx.prod-kamal.conf:98-104, consistent with the surface being intentional rather than accidental, but admin login remains an unthrottled public credential-stuffing target.

Reachability: infra/scout-stack.yml is the live CloudFormation stack (deployment docs at docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-13-cloudwatch-container-logs.md show update-stack runs against it; its ECR/log-group resources match the active deploy workflow). All three findings concern currently-deployed production posture.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced from infra/scout-stack.yml and the deploy pipeline; all paths are live (DEPLOYMENT.md:14 states "All infrastructure is defined in `infra/scout-stack.yml` (CloudFormation) and deployed", and .github/workflows/deploy.yml builds/deploys these exact artifacts).

(a) Topology facts CONFIRMED: DBSubnetGroup at infra/scout-stack.yml:241-247 lists `SubnetIds: [!Ref PublicSubnetA, !Ref PublicSubnetB]`; RedisSubnetGroup at 275-281 same. PublicSubnetA/B (47-67) both have `MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true` and are associated (82-92) to PublicRouteTable whose PublicRoute (74-80) is `DestinationCidrBlock: 0.0.0.0/0 -> !Ref InternetGateway`. RDS has `PubliclyAccessible: false` (268); RDS SG ingress is `SourceSecurityGroupId: !Ref EC2SecurityGroup` only (127-131), Redis SG likewise (141-146). HOWEVER the exposure mechanism is misstated: "a single SG edit adding a CidrIp rule would directly expose the datastores" is wrong. With PubliclyAccessible:false the RDS instance gets no public IP, so its endpoint resolves to a 10.0.x.x address that is unreachable from the internet regardless of SG rules; exposing it also requires a ModifyDBInstance flip of PubliclyAccessible (which the public subnets do make viable — that is the real residual risk). ElastiCache Redis never receives a public endpoint at all; no SG edit can internet-expose it. The defect is real defense-in-depth debt (datastores should be in private subnets), but it is two control-plane changes away for RDS and effectively unreachable for Redis, not one SG edit.

(b) CONFIRMED factually: EC2SecurityGroup ingress tcp/22 `CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0` at infra/scout-stack.yml:102-106 (comment: "SSH (key-only auth, required by Kamal)"); EC2Role ManagedPolicyArns includes `arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore` at 170-171. Both admin paths exist on the single host (EC2Instance 188-197 uses this SG and instance profile). HOWEVER "no functional cost" is misstated for the SSH item: .github/workflows/deploy.yml runs on `ubuntu-latest` (line 18) GitHub-hosted runners with dynamic IPs and deploys via `kamal deploy` over SSH (lines 66-83, ssh-agent setup at 68-71), so tightening 22 to a fixed CIDR would break CI deploys without additional work (GitHub IP-range automation, self-hosted runner, or SSM-based deploy).

(c) CONFIRMED end-to-end: frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf:90-96 `location /admin/ { proxy_pass http://$api_upstream:8000; ... }`; this conf is the deployed one — Dockerfile.frontend:39,48 (`ARG NGINX_CONF`, `COPY ${NGINX_CONF} /etc/nginx/templates/default.conf.template`), config/deploy-frontend.yml:30 `NGINX_CONF: frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf`, .github/workflows/deploy.yml:55 passes the same build-arg, and config/deploy-frontend.yml:41 `host: scout.dimagi.com`. Django side: config/urls.py:82 `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)` and config/settings/base.py:53 `"django.contrib.admin"`. No IP allowlist, non-default URL, or 2FA found in config/settings/production.py or base.py. So https://scout.dimagi.com/admin/ is a public Django admin login surface (session/staff-gated, CSRF-protected, HTTPS — but a credential-stuffing/bruteforce surface with no rate limiting found).
votes.notes: Verdict is PARTIAL, not REFUTED: all cited lines and the DEBT/security classification hold, and sub-claim (c) plus the raw facts of (a) and (b) are fully confirmed. Two corrections: (1) the "single SG edit directly exposes the datastores" escalation in (a) is mechanically wrong — RDS additionally requires a PubliclyAccessible flip, and ElastiCache Redis cannot be internet-exposed at all; the public-subnet placement is defense-in-depth debt whose main effect is making such a flip possible. (2) "no functional cost" in (b) is wrong for SSH: GitHub-hosted runners deploy via Kamal over SSH from dynamic IPs, so closing 0.0.0.0/0 on port 22 has real migration cost (SSM removal, by contrast, genuinely is free if unused). Severity should be read slightly lower than the finding implies for (a)/(b); (c) stands as written.

title: Django admin is an unguarded privileged-write surface: editable state-machine rows can re-arm DROP SCHEMA CASCADE; unthrottled login bypasses allauth/auth rate limiter; plaintext OAuth tokens & client secrets exposed; self-escalation via UserAdmin
claim: The Django admin is mounted unconditionally in production (config/urls.py:82, nginx.prod-kamal.conf proxies /admin/) and is a parallel privileged-write/identity surface with no hardening. (1) Dangerous editable state: TenantSchemaAdmin marks only id/created_at readonly, so state/schema_name/last_accessed_at on TenantSchema (and state/result/progress on MaterializationRun, status on TransformationRun) are fully editable. An operator flipping EXPIRED->ACTIVE without touching last_accessed_at re-arms expire_inactive_schemas into teardown_schema -> DROP SCHEMA CASCADE (operator-keyboard incident-b); stale change-form saves clobber live transitions (lost update); schema_name edits desync the physical schema/_ro role; default delete cascade orphans physical schema+role. (2) /admin/login/ uses stock AdminSite->ModelBackend with no rate limit/lockout/2FA, bypassing both allauth and Scout's own 5/300s per-email limiter (which guards only /api/auth/ and is per-process LocMemCache); production.py adds no admin restriction; target superusers exist via the documented createsuperuser path. (3) allauth auto-registers SocialTokenAdmin/SocialAppAdmin, exposing every user's CommCare/Connect/OCS access+refresh tokens and platform OAuth client secrets in plaintext behind the weakest login. (4) UserAdmin exposes is_staff/is_superuser/user_permissions editable, so any staff with users.change_user self-escalates to superuser. Procrastinate's bundled admin (same process) shows the correct readonly/denied pattern.
chain: config/urls.py:82 admin mounted unconditionally -> TenantSchemaAdmin readonly only id/created_at; operator saves EXPIRED as ACTIVE with stale last_accessed_at -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:538-544 expire_inactive_schemas defers teardown_schema -> DROP SCHEMA CASCADE; AdminSite.login -> ModelBackend no throttle (contrast auth_views.py:109,150 per-email limiter); base.py:65 allauth.socialaccount auto-registers SocialToken/SocialApp admin -> plaintext tokens+client secrets; users/admin.py is_staff/is_superuser editable -> self-escalation

votes.evidence:
Re-traced every link independently.

(A) Admin is live + reachable in prod. config/urls.py:82 `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)` is unconditional; `django.contrib.admin` is in INSTALLED_APPS (config/settings/base.py:53); frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf:90 `location /admin/ { proxy_pass http://$api_upstream:8000; }` proxies it to the Django upstream. No env/DEBUG gate; production.py adds nothing.

(B) Editable state machine re-arms DROP SCHEMA CASCADE. apps/workspaces/admin.py:14 `readonly_fields = ["id", "created_at"]` only -> state/schema_name/last_accessed_at/tenant editable. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:516 `@app.periodic(cron="*/30 * * * *")` expire_inactive_schemas filters `state=SchemaState.ACTIVE, last_accessed_at__lt=cutoff` (tasks.py:538-540) -> sets TEARDOWN -> `teardown_schema.defer_async` (tasks.py:542-544) -> SchemaManager.teardown -> `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {} CASCADE` (apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:198). An admin save flipping EXPIRED->ACTIVE while last_accessed_at stays stale lands the row exactly in this filter, re-arming teardown on the next 30-min tick. MaterializationRunAdmin/TransformationRunAdmin likewise leave state/status editable. Mechanism confirmed; this is the cited operator-keyboard data-loss vector.

(C) Admin login is unthrottled. Scout's limiter (apps/users/rate_limiting.py:5-11) is only wired into auth_views.py:109,150 (the /api/auth/ JSON endpoints). MIDDLEWARE (base.py:83-94) and INSTALLED_APPS contain no django-axes / django-ratelimit; grep for axes/ratelimit/AdminSite override returns nothing. CACHES default is per-process LocMemCache (base.py:318-322, with an explicit code comment that it won't work across workers). AdminSite.login therefore runs stock ModelBackend with no lockout. Superuser targets exist via documented `inv createsuperuser` (README.md:96).

(D) allauth admin auto-registered. .venv allauth/socialaccount/admin.py:68-70 unconditionally `admin.site.register(SocialApp/SocialToken/SocialAccount, ...)`; allauth.socialaccount is in INSTALLED_APPS (base.py:65). SocialTokenAdmin list_display includes truncated_token; the change form exposes the token field. Critically, the EncryptingSocialAccountAdapter (apps/users/adapters.py) only overrides serialize_instance/deserialize_instance, which allauth uses for the SESSION-stashed SocialLogin (allauth models.py:259-265), NOT for DB persistence — the actual write is a plain `self.token.save()` (allauth models.py:315,384). Consumers read `token_obj.token` directly as a usable bearer token (credential_resolver.py:101, token_refresh.py get_user_oauth_tokens, mcp_client.py:85), proving the column stores plaintext. So the admin really does surface plaintext access/refresh tokens; SocialApp secrets are likewise plaintext.

(E) UserAdmin self-escalation. apps/users/admin.py:23-26 places is_staff/is_superuser/user_permissions in an editable fieldset, so a staff user with users.change_user can set is_superuser=True on themselves.

All cited file:line anchors match the actual code. Reachability (live route + prod nginx proxy + scheduled periodic task + existing superusers) is independently confirmed.
votes.notes: Status LATENT and impact data-loss are both appropriate: nothing is failing right now, but the surface re-arms a destructive scheduled task and exposes credentials. Two honest caveats that don't change the verdict: (1) Every vector except the login brute-force is gated behind an authenticated admin session, so this is largely an operator-mistake / stolen-admin-creds threat model, which the finding states plainly (operator-keyboard, "behind the weakest login"). The unthrottled /admin/login/ itself is the one genuinely pre-auth concern. (2) The self-escalation vector (E) needs a non-superuser is_staff account holding users.change_user; Scout's only documented path to is_staff is createsuperuser, which also sets is_superuser, so by default the precondition rarely exists — the finding correctly hedges this as conditional, so it's not misstated, just the lowest-reachability of the five vectors. Bonus: the at-rest plaintext claim is actually understated risk — the adapter named "Encrypting" gives a false sense of protection while only encrypting the transient session copy, not the DB column. If anything, impact could equally be tagged 'security' since the credential-exposure and unthrottled-login facets are more reachable than the operator-only data-loss facet, but data-loss is defensible as the headline.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced every link by reading the actual code and querying the local DB; all hold.

(1) Admin mounted + reachable in prod. config/urls.py:82 `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)` — unconditional, no DEBUG/env guard; production.py adds no admin restriction (read it in full, only TLS/HSTS/cookie hardening). frontend/nginx.prod-kamal.conf lines 90-91 `location /admin/ { proxy_pass http://$api_upstream:8000; }` proxies admin to Django in the Kamal prod deploy. CONFIRMED.

(2) Editable state-machine rows. apps/workspaces/admin.py:14 `readonly_fields = ["id", "created_at"]` — so on TenantSchema (model apps/workspaces/models.py:24-40) the fields `state`, `schema_name`, `tenant`, `last_accessed_at` are all editable in the change form. MaterializationRunAdmin (admin.py:21) marks only id/started_at readonly. apps/transformations/admin.py:25,32 marks only id/started_at readonly so `status` is editable.

(3) Editable state drives DROP SCHEMA CASCADE. tasks.py:538-544 `expire_inactive_schemas` (periodic cron `*/30 * * * *`, line 516) filters `state=ACTIVE, last_accessed_at__lt=cutoff` → sets TEARDOWN → `teardown_schema.defer_async`. teardown_schema (tasks.py:610-620) → SchemaManager.teardown → schema_manager.py:198 `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {} CASCADE`. So an operator who edits a stale-but-live ACTIVE row (or sets state=ACTIVE) without bumping last_accessed_at re-arms the cron into a physical CASCADE drop. CONFIRMED structurally.

(4) Admin login bypasses Scout's limiter. base.py:183-186 AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = ModelBackend + allauth; no django-axes/otp/two_factor/honeypot in INSTALLED_APPS (grepped settings — none). Stock AdminSite.login has no throttle. Scout's own limiter `check_rate_limit`/`record_attempt` (apps/users/rate_limiting.py, 5 attempts / 300s) is called only in apps/users/auth_views.py:109 (login) and :150 (signup) — i.e. only `/api/auth/`. Cache is LocMemCache (base.py:318-322), per-process, with an explicit code comment that it won't work across workers. CONFIRMED.

(5) allauth auto-registers token/secret admin. .venv allauth/socialaccount/admin.py:68-69 `admin.site.register(SocialApp, SocialAppAdmin)` / `register(SocialToken, SocialTokenAdmin)`; INSTALLED_APPS includes allauth.socialaccount (base.py:65). DB check: SocialApp.secret is stored PLAINTEXT (length 128, prefix 'U2M4JwSw'); SocialToken.token is also plaintext at rest (length 30). Note: Scout's EncryptingSocialAccountAdapter encrypts only via serialize_instance/deserialize_instance — the session-stash path — NOT the DB write (allauth SocialLogin.save→token.save() is a plain model save), so tokens are in fact plaintext in the DB and the admin surfaces them. CONFIRMED.

(6) UserAdmin self-escalation. apps/users/admin.py:20-28 fieldsets expose `is_staff`, `is_superuser`, `groups`, `user_permissions` as editable; any staff user with `users.change_user` can set is_superuser=True. CONFIRMED (standard Django UserAdmin exposure, no override).
votes.notes:
One imprecision in the finding's headline narrative: the specific "EXPIRED->ACTIVE re-arms DROP SCHEMA CASCADE" example is the weakest data-loss vector — an EXPIRED TenantSchema has ALREADY had its physical schema dropped (teardown_schema sets EXPIRED only after the DROP succeeds, tasks.py:656), so flipping it back to ACTIVE and letting the cron re-tear-down just runs `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS` on an already-absent schema (no new loss). The genuine data-loss comes from the broader, correctly-stated mechanism: editing a still-live ACTIVE row's state/last_accessed_at, the lost-update clobber of a concurrent transition, schema_name desync, or default delete-cascade — all enabled because state-machine fields wired to a CASCADE drop are freely editable. So the structural defect and every other sub-claim are fully confirmed; only the illustrative incident framing is slightly off.

Reachability/status: LATENT is correct — nothing fires today without an operator action or an admin-credential compromise. Reachable in production (admin mounted + nginx-proxied), gated only by a superuser/staff password with no lockout/2FA/throttle; superusers exist via the documented createsuperuser path. Impact is correctly tagged data-loss (worst case = CASCADE drop), but it is simultaneously a security finding (plaintext OAuth tokens + client secrets exposed, privilege self-escalation) — a multi-headed gap rather than a single bug.

title: merge_users OR-propagates is_staff/is_superuser from the deleted duplicate onto the canonical account, invisible at the confirmation prompt
claim: apps/users/services/merge.py:235-240 grants the canonical user is_staff/is_superuser if the duplicate had them. Cleaning up a stale createsuperuser dev artifact by merging it into a normal OAuth user silently promotes that user to production superuser (full admin capabilities). The merge_duplicate_users command's printed plan summarizes repoint counts but does not surface the privilege escalation at the y/N prompt. Given the 2026-06 prod merge of exactly such an artifact, this is plausibly already production state.
chain: merge_duplicate_users.py handle() -> merge_users(canonical, duplicate) -> merge.py:235-240 canonical.is_staff/is_superuser |= duplicate flags; _print_plan omits the flag change

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently:

1. OR-propagation exists exactly as claimed. /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/apps/users/services/merge.py:235-240 in `_merge_user_fields`: `if duplicate.is_staff and not canonical.is_staff: canonical.is_staff = True` and `if duplicate.is_superuser and not canonical.is_superuser: canonical.is_superuser = True`. Called from `merge_users` at merge.py:332 (`report.field_changes = _merge_user_fields(canonical, duplicate)`) inside `transaction.atomic()`, and documented as intended in the docstring (merge.py:268 "staff/super OR").

2. Invisible at the prompt — confirmed, and worse than stated. The command's plan preview calls `merge_users(..., dry_run=True)` (merge_duplicate_users.py:48), and the dry-run branch of `merge_users` returns at merge.py:329 BEFORE `_merge_user_fields` is ever called — `field_changes` is always `{}` in the previewed MergeReport, so the flag change is structurally absent from the plan, not merely unprinted. Independently, `_print_plan` (merge_duplicate_users.py:91-104) prints only repoint/conflict counts (socialaccounts, emails, tenant, workspace) and never references `report.field_changes`. The y/N prompt (merge_duplicate_users.py:55-61) shows only "About to merge N duplicate(s)".

3. Reachability — live on two paths: (a) operator command `manage.py merge_duplicate_users` (merge_duplicate_users.py:35-70, handle() -> merge_users); (b) auto-merge on OAuth login via `reconcile_existing_user_on_login` (apps/users/signals.py:119 `merge_users(canonical=canonical, duplicate=user)`), wired to allauth's `pre_social_login` in apps/users/apps.py:24. The signal path merges the email-less OAuth login user as *duplicate*, so escalation there requires the OAuth-only row to hold staff/superuser flags (unlikely but possible).

4. Post-merge, only the merge is logged via `logger.info` (merge.py:349-354) which does include the MergeReport repr with field_changes — but that is after the irreversible write, not at the decision point.
votes.notes: Mechanism nuance (does not change the verdict): in the default-selection scenario the finding describes ("merging a dev artifact into a normal OAuth user"), `select_canonical` (merge.py:104-117) ranks has_usable_password first, so a createsuperuser artifact with a usable password would be chosen as CANONICAL — the 235-240 OR path would not fire; instead the OAuth user's identity gets repointed onto the surviving superuser account (equivalent escalation, different mechanism, equally invisible at the prompt). The literal 235-240 path fires when the artifact ends up as duplicate: operator forces `--canonical-id` to the OAuth user, or the artifact has an unusable password (`createsuperuser --noinput` without password env). The "plausibly already production state" claim is consistent with the known 2026-06 operator merge of exactly such an artifact but is not verifiable from code; whether prod escalated depends on which row was canonical and on flag state at merge time — worth a one-off prod query (`User.objects.filter(is_superuser=True)`) to confirm. Suggested fix: print field_changes in the plan (requires computing `_merge_user_fields` effects in the dry-run branch) and/or require an explicit `--allow-privilege-escalation` flag when duplicate.is_staff/is_superuser would change canonical.is_staff/is_superuser.

votes.evidence: Re-traced independently. (1) Mechanism: apps/users/services/merge.py:235-240 reads `if duplicate.is_staff and not canonical.is_staff: canonical.is_staff = True` and `if duplicate.is_superuser and not canonical.is_superuser: canonical.is_superuser = True`, applied inside `_merge_user_fields` which is called at merge.py:332 within the write transaction. Intentional and test-pinned: tests/test_merge_users_service.py:61-74 `test_field_level_merge_ors_staff_and_superuser_flags` asserts exactly this. (2) Invisibility at prompt — stronger than the finding states: the dry_run branch of merge_users (merge.py:285-329) returns at line 329 WITHOUT ever calling `_merge_user_fields`, so `report.field_changes` is always empty in the planning pass the command uses (merge_duplicate_users.py:48 calls `merge_users(..., dry_run=True)`); independently, `_print_plan` (merge_duplicate_users.py:91-104) prints only socialaccount/email/tenant/workspace repoint counts and never field_changes. So the flag escalation is invisible both at the y/N prompt (lines 55-61) and under --dry-run. (3) Reachability: two live entry points — the operator command, and `reconcile_existing_user_on_login` wired via `pre_social_login.connect` at apps/users/apps.py:24; in the signal path (apps/users/signals.py:119) the OAuth-logging-in user is the duplicate, so a privileged email-less user logging in escalates the email-owning canonical with no operator in the loop at all.
votes.notes: Two corrections/refinements, neither changing the verdict. (a) The finding's headline scenario (artifact merged INTO the OAuth user, flags OR'd) requires `--canonical-id` forcing the OAuth user as canonical: `select_canonical` (merge.py:110-117) prefers usable-password + oldest, so a createsuperuser artifact would win canonical by default and lines 235-240 would not fire — but the default direction yields an equivalent escalation (the surviving account IS the superuser artifact, now owning the OAuth SocialAccount, so the OAuth human controls a superuser account). Either direction ends with the OAuth identity attached to a superuser account, and neither is surfaced at the prompt. (b) "Plausibly already production state" is unverifiable from code; the 2026-06 prod merge did occur per project memory but the resulting flag state can't be confirmed here — treat as a follow-up audit item (check the merged prod user's is_staff/is_superuser), not an established fact. Severity-wise the prompt-invisibility is slightly understated: even --dry-run cannot reveal it because the dry_run branch skips _merge_user_fields entirely.

title: Admin registration inverted: dangerous raw rows fully editable while every model operators need (ThreadJob, WorkspaceViewSchema, memberships, connections) is absent; RecipeAdmin manages vestigial RecipeStep and omits live Recipe.prompt
claim: Workspace, WorkspaceTenant, WorkspaceMembership, WorkspaceViewSchema, ThreadJob, Thread, Tenant, TenantMembership, TenantConnection and TenantMetadata have no admin registration; every recent incident (zombie jobs, stuck Preparing…, EXPIRED resurrection, cascade-FAILED view schemas) required inspecting/repairing exactly these rows via psql or bespoke commands — while TenantSchema.state sits fully editable. The shipped admin surface maximizes operator harm and minimizes operator help; read-only registrations (procrastinate's bundled admin is the in-process template) would invert both. Separately, RecipeAdmin's fieldsets omit the live Recipe.prompt (the only field the runner executes) and are built around the dead RecipeStep model (inline + ModelAdmin + step_count/step_progress), inviting operators to create rows nothing reads; soft-deleted recipes/artifacts are invisible/unrestorable because the soft-delete manager is the default manager.
chain: grep of all @admin.register/admin.site.register in apps/ shows missing operator models + editable TenantSchema.state; recipes/models.py:53 prompt consumed at runner.py:195 but admin.py:39-63 fieldsets omit it; admin.py:11-17,74-90,166-171 build on dead RecipeStep

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every link from a full grep of admin registrations to the live model/runner consumers.

MISSING OPERATOR MODELS — full grep of @admin.register/admin.site.register across apps/ yields only: Artifact, Recipe, RecipeStep, RecipeRun, User, TransformationAsset/Run/AssetRun, TableKnowledge, KnowledgeEntry, AgentLearning, TenantSchema, MaterializationRun. The incident-critical models all exist as real Django models yet have NO admin: chat has no admin.py at all, so Thread (apps/chat/models.py:8) and ThreadJob (apps/chat/models.py:51) are unregistered; Tenant (apps/users/models.py:110), TenantConnection (:150), TenantMembership (:195) unregistered; Workspace (apps/workspaces/models.py:109), WorkspaceTenant (:169), WorkspaceMembership (:187), WorkspaceViewSchema (:214), TenantMetadata (:258) unregistered.

TENANTSCHEMA.STATE EDITABLE — apps/workspaces/admin.py:14 'readonly_fields = ["id", "created_at"]'; `state` (models.py:34) is absent from readonly_fields and there is no fieldsets/exclude, so it renders editable in the change form. CONFIRMED.

RECIPEADMIN OMITS LIVE prompt — Recipe.prompt defined at apps/recipes/models.py:53; consumed by render_prompt() (models.py:143 `rendered = self.prompt`) which the runner calls at apps/recipes/services/runner.py:195 `prompt = self.recipe.render_prompt(...)`; serializer exposes it (apps/recipes/api/serializers.py:56,76). RecipeAdmin.fieldsets (admin.py:39-63) lists id/name/description/workspace, variables, is_shared/is_public/share_token, created_by/created_at/updated_at — NO `prompt`. CONFIRMED.

RECIPESTEP DEAD — grep across apps/recipes shows RecipeStep / prompt_template only in models.py (218-278), admin.py, and migrations; zero references in api/serializers.py, api/views.py, or services/runner.py. admin.py still builds on it: RecipeStepInline (11-17), @admin.register(RecipeStep) (74-90), step_count via obj.steps.count() (65-67), step_progress via obj.recipe.steps.count() (166-171). CONFIRMED — operators can create RecipeStep rows nothing reads.

SOFT-DELETE INVISIBILITY — apps/recipes/models.py:103 `objects = RecipeSoftDeleteManager()` (filters is_deleted=False at :17) is the default manager; :104 all_objects exists but RecipeAdmin defines no get_queryset override, so soft-deleted recipes are invisible/unrestorable in admin. Same pattern for ArtifactAdmin: apps/artifacts/models.py:132 `objects = SoftDeleteManager()` (filters is_deleted=False at :18), admin.py defines no get_queryset override. CONFIRMED.

REACHABILITY — admin is live: config/urls.py:82 `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)` and config/settings/base.py:53 lists `django.contrib.admin`. The harmful surface (editable TenantSchema.state, RecipeStep CRUD) and the missing-model gap are both reachable today.
votes.notes: Verdict CONFIRMED on every sub-claim. Two minor framing caveats (not refutations): (1) The finding's title says operators repaired "exactly these rows via psql" during recent incidents — I verified the MODELS are unregistered and the incidents in memory (zombie ThreadJob, stuck Preparing, EXPIRED resurrection, cascade-FAILED view schemas) map exactly onto ThreadJob/TenantSchema/WorkspaceViewSchema, but I did not independently re-verify the historical repair method (out of scope of the code). (2) Impact is correctly velocity/DEBT (nothing is broken at runtime), but the editable TenantSchema.state is a privileged WRITE path: an operator save could push a schema into an inconsistent lifecycle state (e.g. mark EXPIRED→READY without the underlying schema existing), which trends toward correctness/data-integrity rather than pure velocity. I'd leave status DEBT but note the latent write-corruption angle. The procrastinate-bundled-admin remedy reference is plausible (Procrastinate is the task queue per CLAUDE.md) though I did not open its admin module. The core mechanism, files, line numbers, and reachability all check out.

title: AgentLearningAdmin confidence_badge relies on removed allow_tags; renders escaped literal HTML
claim: knowledge/admin.py:134-144 returns a raw f-string span and sets confidence_badge.allow_tags=True; allow_tags was removed in Django 2.0, so on Django 5 the list column renders the literal escaped '<span style=...>' text instead of a colored badge. Purely cosmetic; the adjacent admins use format_html correctly.
chain: knowledge/admin.py:134-144 raw f-string span + allow_tags=True -> Django 5 escapes -> literal HTML text in list column

votes.evidence: Re-traced the full chain in apps/knowledge/admin.py. AgentLearningAdmin.confidence_badge (lines 134-142) returns a plain f-string: `return f'{score:.0%}'`, then line 144 sets `confidence_badge.allow_tags = True`. This method is registered in list_display (line 87 of the AgentLearning changelist columns). Reachability confirmed: django.contrib.admin is in INSTALLED_APPS (config/settings/base.py:53), the admin is mounted at config/urls.py:82 `path("admin/", admin.site.urls)`, and AgentLearning is @admin.register'd. confidence_badge is a live changelist column, so any staff user opening the AgentLearning list view hits this path. Django version verified: pyproject.toml requires `django>=5.0` and `uv run python -c "import django"` reports Django 5.2.11. allow_tags is a dead no-op on Django 5: I introspected django.contrib.admin.templatetags.admin_list.items_for_result (the changelist column renderer) and 'allow_tags' is NOT referenced there. allow_tags was removed in Django 2.0. I also empirically reproduced the escaping: the f-string is not a SafeString (isinstance(...,SafeString) == False), so Django's conditional_escape produces `<span style="color: green; font-weight: bold;">85%</span>` — i.e. the literal escaped HTML renders in the column instead of a colored badge. grep confirmed no change_list_template override or custom items_for_result anywhere in apps/config/templates that could resurrect allow_tags behavior. Adjacent-admin claim substantially correct: format_html is used correctly at apps/recipes/admin.py:160 and apps/artifacts/admin.py:141; this confidence_badge is the only method relying on the removed allow_tags.
votes.notes: Severity (COSMETIC / velocity) is accurate: escaped text is visually broken but carries no data-loss or security risk (in fact escaping makes it inert). One minor imprecision in the finding's wording: "the adjacent admins use format_html correctly" — the literal adjacent method in the SAME file (KnowledgeEntryAdmin.tags_display) returns plain text (no HTML, no format_html), so it is safe by being plain text rather than by format_html. The actual format_html exemplars live in apps/recipes/admin.py:160 and apps/artifacts/admin.py:141. This does not affect the verdict. The fix is trivial: return format_html('{:.0%}', color, score) and drop the allow_tags line.

title: setup_oauth_apps composes wrong env-var names for Google/GitHub (double _OAUTH_); skip message and docstring name two further spellings, so Google/GitHub can never bootstrap via this command
claim: env_prefix is 'GOOGLE_OAUTH'/'GITHUB_OAUTH' and the lookup appends '_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID', so the command reads GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID / GITHUB_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID; the skip message prints '{prefix}_CLIENT_ID not set' (a third spelling) and the docstring promises GOOGLE_OAUTH_* / COMMCARE_CONNECT_OAUTH_* (actual: CONNECT_OAUTH_* per deploy.yml). An operator following the docstring or the error message can never bootstrap Google/GitHub via this command — it silently skips forever. Unused by the current prod deploy (only COMMCARE/CONNECT/OCS vars passed) and recoverable by hand-creating SocialApp in admin, hence LATENT.
chain: setup_oauth_apps.py:16-22 env_prefix='GOOGLE_OAUTH' -> :46-47 reads GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID -> always empty -> :50 skip message names GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID; docstring :3-4 a third set; deploy.yml:47-52 real var names

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced from the file at apps/users/management/commands/setup_oauth_apps.py.

(1) PROVIDERS list, lines 20-21: ("google", "Google", "GOOGLE_OAUTH") and ("github", "GitHub", "GITHUB_OAUTH"). The three working providers use BARE prefixes: line 17 "COMMCARE", line 18 "CONNECT", line 19 "OCS".

(2) Line 46: client_id = os.environ.get(f"{env_prefix}_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID", ""). f-string composition for Google yields literally GOOGLE_OAUTH + _OAUTH_CLIENT_ID = "GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID" (double _OAUTH_); GitHub yields "GITHUB_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID". grep across the entire repo (yml/sh/py/md/env.example/secrets) finds NO variable with that double spelling — it can never be populated. CONFIRMED.

(3) Cross-check that bare-prefix is the correct pattern: COMMCARE -> COMMCARE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID == config/deploy.yml:47; CONNECT -> CONNECT_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID == deploy.yml:49 and .kamal/secrets:11; OCS -> OCS_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID == deploy.yml:51 and .env.example:35. So the malformed prefix is unique to Google/GitHub.

(4) Line 50 skip message: f"  skip   {name} ({env_prefix}_CLIENT_ID not set)" -> for Google prints "GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID not set" — a THIRD spelling, different from what line 46 actually reads (GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID). CONFIRMED.

(5) Docstring lines 3-4: "Reads COMMCARE_OAUTH_*, COMMCARE_CONNECT_OAUTH_*, GOOGLE_OAUTH_*, and GITHUB_OAUTH_*". The "COMMCARE_CONNECT_OAUTH_*" name does not match the code, which reads CONNECT_OAUTH_* (line 18 prefix "CONNECT", matching deploy.yml:49). The "GOOGLE_OAUTH_*" docstring promise also mismatches the code's actual GOOGLE_OAUTH_OAUTH_* read. CONFIRMED.

Reachability: command is LIVE — invoked unconditionally at container startup in docker-entrypoint.sh:13 (python manage.py setup_oauth_apps --domain "$OAUTH_DOMAIN") and documented for prod in DEPLOYMENT.md:235. Google/GitHub providers are enabled in INSTALLED_APPS (config/settings/base.py:66-67), so support intent is real. However prod deploy.yml only passes COMMCARE/CONNECT/OCS secrets (lines 47-52), no Google/GitHub vars — so today the command always reaches the line-50 skip branch for Google/GitHub regardless of the bug. The defect only bites an operator who attempts to bootstrap Google/GitHub via env vars per the docstring/error message; their correctly-named GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID would be ignored and silently skipped forever. base.py:224 documents the recovery path ("Configure client IDs and secrets via Django admin at /admin/socialaccount/socialapp/"), confirming the hand-create escape hatch.
votes.notes: All four sub-claims (double _OAUTH_ read, third-spelling skip message, docstring GOOGLE_OAUTH_* mismatch, docstring COMMCARE_CONNECT_OAUTH_* vs actual CONNECT_OAUTH_*) reproduced exactly. Line ranges in the finding match the file precisely (PROVIDERS 16-22, read 46-47, skip 50, docstring 3-4, deploy.yml 47-52). LATENT/velocity classification is correct: the path is wired to a live startup command but Google/GitHub are unused in current prod (no env vars passed), and the failure mode is a silent no-op recoverable via admin, not a runtime break or security/data issue. No correction needed.

title: backfill_readonly_roles aborts on first Django-vs-physical schema drift, leaving later schemas role-less (fail-closed query outage); selects dead MATERIALIZING state; no per-schema error handling, dry-run, or view-role default privileges
claim: The SET-ROLE-defense backstop command has no try/except around its per-schema loops: any ACTIVE TenantSchema row whose physical schema is missing (drift producers: MCP teardown_schema, purge_synced_data) makes GRANT USAGE raise InvalidSchemaName and aborts the whole command, so every schema after it never gets its _ro role and all their MCP queries fail at SET ROLE until re-run after manual repair. The command's stated idempotency IS true (pg_roles check + DuplicateObject suppression + idempotent GRANTs + autocommit). Lesser issues: it selects the dead MATERIALIZING state; the view-schema branch grants are point-in-time (no ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES for the view role on tenant schemas) so it cannot repair a workspace between rematerialization and view rebuild; no --dry-run/logging unlike purge_synced_data.
chain: backfill_readonly_roles.py:34-36/43-63 loops with no exception handling -> GRANT USAGE on dropped schema raises InvalidSchemaName -> handle() aborts -> remaining ACTIVE schemas never backfilled -> query.py:44 SET ROLE <schema>_ro fails (fail-closed outage)

votes.evidence:
Primary chain CONFIRMED by independent re-trace:
- backfill_readonly_roles.py:29-68: the ONLY try block is paired with `finally` (lines 29/66) for cursor/conn cleanup — no `except`. The per-schema loops (`for ts in tenant_schemas:` L34, `for vs in view_schemas:` L42, `for ts in tenant_schemas_for_ws:` L50) have zero exception handling.
- _backfill_schema (L70-80) -> _create_readonly_role (schema_manager.py:588-623): L609-614 issues `GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA {Identifier} TO {Identifier}` with a real Identifier and no IF EXISTS. On a dropped schema, PostgreSQL raises InvalidSchemaName (3F000); I confirmed `psycopg.errors.InvalidSchemaName` exists. This propagates out of handle(), aborting the command -> all later ACTIVE schemas never get their _ro role.
- autocommit confirmed: get_managed_db_connection() at schema_manager.py:43 returns `psycopg.connect(url, autocommit=True)`, so prior schemas' grants are committed but later ones are skipped (role-less), exactly as claimed.
- Consequence reachable: mcp_server/services/query.py:44 runs `SET ROLE {ctx.readonly_role}` (=`{schema}_ro`) before every query; a missing role fails fail-closed. `query` is a live MCP agent tool (apps/agents/graph/base.py MCP_TOOL_NAMES includes "query").

DRIFT-PRODUCER claim is PARTLY WRONG (basis for PARTIAL):
- purge_synced_data.py is NOT a valid drift producer: after teardown it does `TenantSchema.objects.all().delete()` (L59) — it removes the rows entirely, so it never leaves an ACTIVE row with a missing physical schema for the backfill to trip on.
- teardown_schema TASK (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:610-663) is also NOT a drift producer: success path marks EXPIRED (L656); failure path (manager.teardown raised => DROP failed => schema still exists) re-sets ACTIVE (L629). Neither yields ACTIVE-row + missing-schema.
- HOWEVER the MCP tool teardown_schema (mcp_server/server.py:802-865) IS a genuine drift producer: it loops over ACTIVE/non-TEARDOWN TenantSchema rows (L853-858) calling `mgr.ateardown(ts)`, which only does `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS ... CASCADE` (schema_manager.py:480-484) and NEVER updates the Django row state. So after this tool runs, TenantSchema rows stay ACTIVE while their physical schemas are gone. It is wired as a live agent tool (base.py:73). provision() (schema_manager.py:74-76) also documents external drops leaving records ACTIVE. So the drift PREMISE holds, but the finding names the wrong producers.

SECONDARY claims CONFIRMED:
- "selects dead MATERIALIZING": grep over apps/ + mcp_server/ found NO write of SchemaState.MATERIALIZING anywhere — only the enum def (models.py:18) and read-filters. So no TenantSchema is ever in MATERIALIZING; including it in the backfill filter (L32) is harmless dead code, exactly as the finding frames it.
- view-role default privileges: build_view_schema (schema_manager.py:391-405) grants the view role only point-in-time GRANT USAGE + GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES on each tenant schema — no ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES for the view role on tenant schemas (the only ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES is in _create_readonly_role for the schema's OWN role, L615-622). The backfill view branch (L50-62) likewise only does point-in-time GRANTs. So neither durably repairs a workspace between tenant rematerialization (new tables) and view rebuild. Confirmed.
- no --dry-run/logging: confirmed; purge_synced_data has --confirm + dry-run (L20-44), backfill has neither.
- stated idempotency IS true: pg_roles existence check (L597-601) + contextlib.suppress(DuplicateObject) (L603) + idempotent GRANTs + autocommit. Confirmed.

Reachability/severity: the backfill command is invoked ONLY in tests (tests/test_backfill_readonly_roles.py) — no cron/Procfile/scheduled wiring. It is a manual operator backstop, and its tests use mocked cursors that never exercise the InvalidSchemaName abort. LATENT is the correct status.
votes.notes: Net: the defect (no per-schema error handling -> first drift aborts the whole command -> later schemas left role-less -> fail-closed at SET ROLE) is REAL and I reproduced the chain. I downgrade to PARTIAL only because the finding's named drift producers are inaccurate: purge_synced_data deletes the rows (no drift) and the teardown_schema TASK keeps Django state consistent (EXPIRED/ACTIVE). The actual live drift producer is the MCP teardown_schema TOOL (server.py:802-865) which drops physical schemas via ateardown() without ever updating TenantSchema.state, plus generic external drops (documented at schema_manager.py:74-76). All other primary and secondary claims (autocommit fail-after-first, InvalidSchemaName, dead MATERIALIZING write, missing view-role ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES, no dry-run, true idempotency, fail-closed SET ROLE consequence) are independently verified. Impact correctly characterized as correctness/LATENT.

title: users migration 0004 dedup can cascade-delete TenantMetadata on the discarded duplicate membership (historical, one-time)
claim: Full RunPython/RunSQL sweep found exactly three data migrations (users 0003/0004/0007; zero RunSQL anywhere). 0003 is trivially safe. 0004 keeps the earliest-created TenantMembership and deletes later duplicates; TenantMetadata.tenant_membership is a CASCADE OneToOne created before this migration (workspaces 0001 2026-03-17 vs users 0004 2026-04-22), so discovery metadata attached to a later duplicate was deleted while the kept membership may have had none. Same earliest-row-over-row-with-data pattern as the known merge-conflict metadata loss, at an earlier site. Already executed; relevant as pattern evidence only.
chain: users 0004 keeps earliest TenantMembership, deletes later dups -> TenantMetadata.tenant_membership CASCADE OneToOne (workspaces 0001 predates) -> metadata on a later duplicate cascade-deleted

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain and reproduced it; the defect is real, but one cited file location is wrong.

1. users 0004 dedup logic confirmed verbatim (apps/users/migrations/0004_deduplicate_tenant_memberships.py:14-27): for each duplicate (user_id, tenant_id) group it `keep = ...order_by("created_at").first()` then `TenantMembership.objects.filter(...).exclude(id=keep.id).delete()` — keeps earliest-created, hard-deletes the rest. Runs via `migrations.RunPython(deduplicate_memberships, ...noop)` (line 36).

2. The CASCADE OneToOne is REAL but created in workspaces 0002, NOT 0001 as the finding states. workspaces 0001_initial.py:49-83 creates TenantMetadata WITHOUT the tenant_membership field. The field is added in apps/workspaces/migrations/0002_initial.py:18-26: `AddField(model_name="tenantmetadata", name="tenant_membership", field=OneToOneField(on_delete=CASCADE, related_name="metadata", to="users.tenantmembership"))`. Current model agrees (apps/workspaces/models.py:266-270, on_delete=models.CASCADE).

3. Timeline still holds: `git log` dates workspaces 0002 at 2026-03-17 (commit "rename projects app to workspaces #89") vs users 0004 at 2026-04-22. The DB-level CASCADE FK constraint thus exists before 0004 runs. Because the cascade is enforced by Postgres, the historical-state `.delete()` in 0004 cascade-deletes any TenantMetadata attached to a discarded later-duplicate membership — regardless of Django app load ordering.

4. Why dedup exists / why dups were possible: TenantMembership has `unique_together = [["user","tenant"]]` (apps/users/models.py:226); 0004 is the cleanup that removes pre-existing duplicates. created_at is `auto_now_add` (line 223), so the earliest-created keeper is well-defined.

5. Sweep claim verified: `grep -rln RunPython apps/*/migrations/` returns exactly users 0003, 0004, 0007; `grep -rln RunSQL` returns nothing. So "exactly three data migrations, zero RunSQL" is accurate.

6. Reachability/status: TenantMembership (apps/users/models.py:195) and TenantMetadata are live models, but 0004 is a one-time already-applied data migration (noop reverse). It is correctly classified DEBT/pattern-evidence, not a live path.
votes.notes: PARTIAL rather than CONFIRMED solely because of a citation error: the finding attributes the CASCADE OneToOne to "workspaces 0001 (2026-03-17)". The model creation is in 0001, but the tenant_membership CASCADE FK is actually added in workspaces 0002_initial.py (AddField). Both are dated 2026-03-17 / same commit, so the "predates users 0004 (2026-04-22)" conclusion and the entire cascade-delete chain are unaffected. Mechanism, severity (DEBT/correctness), replication (1, historical one-time), and the "same earliest-row-over-row-with-data pattern as the known merge-conflict metadata loss" framing are all accurate. Recommend updating the cited file from `apps/workspaces/migrations/0001_initial.py` to `0002_initial.py:18-26` (the runtime model ref apps/workspaces/models.py:266-270 is correct as-is).
